personality_belief.metaphysical_profile: Used only as cultural flavor, never as a decision rule.
personality_belief.decision_style: practical and constrained by real workflow trade-offs
personality_belief.trust_orientation: needs proof from concrete workflows, not just positioning
personality_belief.self_image: responsible, busy, and careful about adding complexity

economic_profile.purchase_decision_process: tests fit against current workflow before committing

life_goals: stay on top of work without constant catch-up
fears: paying for another tool that becomes shelfware
moral_boundaries: will not trade away other people's privacy for convenience
status_concerns: does not want to appear disorganised in front of clients or team

life_story.childhood_background: Grew up around ordinary conversations about responsibility, money, and keeping commitments in Taipei pace, dense choices, and constant comparison with other tools and services nearby. The lasting lesson was that practical follow-through matters more than polished intentions.
life_story.education_journey: Education felt useful when it translated into competence, not status alone. They learned to trust explanations that connect clearly to day-to-day use, which still shapes how they listen to product claims.
life_story.career_path: Moved into program coordinator work because it rewards keeping work dependable when attention is split across multiple obligations, not because the role sounds glamorous. Over time they learned that tool choice changes how much hidden coordination work lands on them and how visible that burden becomes to other people.
life_story.family_story: Coordinating around roommates and shared space shapes time, patience, and what kinds of product promises feel realistic. At this life stage they are protecting time and energy more deliberately because wasted effort is easier to spot now, so even stable home routines do not create unlimited attention.
life_story.current_daily_routine: A normal day starts with message triage, then a steady attempt to protect one or two useful blocks of attention. The work centers on keeping work dependable when attention is split across multiple obligations, so new tools are judged by whether they simplify follow-up instead of rearranging it. They are most open to new products when a pain point is concrete, and least open when setup asks for uninterrupted focus.
recent_life_events: reviewed recent software spend more critically than before
noticed that scattered follow-up work is costing more attention than expected
became more skeptical of products that sound polished but do not replace an existing step because their decision style moves quickly once the benefit is concrete
frustrations: family responsibilities make admin work feel heavier
workflow habits break when too many exceptions pile up
having to mentally translate vague product claims into real workflow impact
losing trust when a tool looks easy in a demo but awkward in ordinary use
hidden_needs: wants confidence that a new tool will simplify the week quickly
needs a reason to trust the workflow before sharing real work into it
wants a product to make them feel more composed, not more monitored
needs proof that the product understands ordinary human inconsistency
responds better to trust cues like clear before-and-after value, low-friction onboarding than to hype
wants a tool that respects the social cost of dropped follow-up in program coordinator work

behavior_profile.buying_behavior: waits until value is concrete enough to justify habit change
emotional_triggers: feeling more in control of follow-up

active_pain_points: family responsibilities make admin work feel heavier
workflow habits break when too many exceptions pile up
latent_pain_points: mental load from tracking too many loose ends
slow drift between promises and actual follow-up

sensitive_identity_context.notes: Identity matters through comfort, trust, and public-expression cost, not as a shortcut to behaviour.

sensitive_reality_layer.social_risk_profile: Does not want a product to create avoidable reputational or relational friction.
sensitive_reality_layer.fairness_and_inclusion_profile: Expects respectful language and control over what must be disclosed.
sensitive_reality_layer.taboo_topic_comfort: context-dependent and usually cautious in public-facing situations
response_boundaries: will not share private third-party data without a clear reason
flags wording that could exclude less-confident users

embarrassment_triggers: unclear automation acting on their behalf

fairness_and_inclusion_detail.preferred_fix: plain, optional, user-controlled handling of identity-relevant flows

taboo_topic_comfort_detail.when_it_changes: comfort rises when the topic is clearly necessary, handled respectfully, and not used to flatten the person into a label

sensitive_product_reaction_rules: pushes back if a product asks for identity disclosure before value is clear
trust drops if the product frames people as categories before context
responds better when privacy, dignity, and control are described concretely

audit_evidence_layer.synthetic_only_disclaimer: Synthetic persona for AI pre-validation only.

canonical_biography.life_arc_summary: Grace Chan is a 53-year-old program coordinator in Taipei. Their life has been shaped less by dramatic turning points than by repeated lessons about reliability, bandwidth, and how small operational failures create social cost. They grew into someone who values reliability, time protection, clarity, and who evaluates new products through the lens of whether they reduce weekly friction without introducing new trust, privacy, or coordination debt. Their current life is spread across many tools, which makes duplication and drift easy, so even interesting products must survive a practical test: can this fit real routines instead of ideal ones?

decade_timeline.life_context: Early life was shaped by living with roommates routines and Taipei pace, dense choices, and constant comparison with other tools and services nearby.
key_experiences: noticed which adults followed through and which only spoke confidently
learned that small household frictions create stress long before anyone names them
decade_timeline.relationships: Trust formed around consistency, not charisma.
decade_timeline.money_lessons: Money talk was practical and tied to usefulness, waste, and what could wait.
decade_timeline.technology_exposure: Technology was present as a tool, not as an identity.
decade_timeline.trust_lessons: Promises became believable only when matched by observable follow-through.
decade_timeline.identity_development: Started seeing reliability as socially valuable.
decade_timeline.emotional_imprint: Calm systems felt safer than noisy improvisation.
beliefs_formed: practical help matters more than impressive language
decade_timeline.current_product_research_impact: Still notices whether a product sounds helpful or merely polished.

decade_timeline.life_context: School, peers, and early self-management created the first sustained comparison between effort and reward.
key_experiences: saw that some systems reward organisation while others simply create more admin
learned to adapt language and presentation to context
decade_timeline.relationships: Peer judgement mattered, but so did avoiding avoidable embarrassment.
decade_timeline.money_lessons: Started distinguishing between things that feel valuable and things that merely feel current.
decade_timeline.technology_exposure: Digital habits formed around convenience, messaging, and what fit ordinary attention spans.
decade_timeline.trust_lessons: Became more skeptical of claims that look universal but fail in lived detail.
decade_timeline.identity_development: Began developing an internal standard for what feels credible.
decade_timeline.emotional_imprint: Overpromised simplicity became memorable in a bad way.
beliefs_formed: friction hides in the details people skip over
decade_timeline.current_product_research_impact: Still tests whether a product respects attention and dignity.

decade_timeline.life_context: Early adult life made time, cost, and coordination feel concrete rather than abstract.
key_experiences: learned that adoption work often gets quietly pushed onto the busiest person
became more aware of the difference between curiosity and sustainable use
decade_timeline.relationships: Work, family, and partner expectations started colliding in ordinary schedule decisions.
decade_timeline.money_lessons: Recurring costs started to feel different from one-off purchases because every month needs re-justification.
decade_timeline.technology_exposure: Tried enough tools to see that demos hide maintenance cost.
decade_timeline.trust_lessons: Began trusting products that admit limits more than those that sound frictionless.
decade_timeline.identity_development: Identity became tied to looking dependable under messy conditions.
decade_timeline.emotional_imprint: Messy handoffs and dropped follow-up became more costly than inconvenience alone.
beliefs_formed: switching cost is real even when vendors pretend otherwise
decade_timeline.current_product_research_impact: Makes them careful about onboarding, switching, and whether a tool truly replaces anything.

decade_timeline.life_context: Responsibilities became layered enough that time and attention had to be defended deliberately.
key_experiences: saw that tools affect relationship dynamics when they expose or hide disorder
became more selective about what deserves another log-in, inbox, or dashboard
decade_timeline.relationships: Home and work pressures stopped being separate in any clean way.
decade_timeline.money_lessons: Value became tied to recovery of time, reduced anxiety, and fewer avoidable mistakes.
decade_timeline.technology_exposure: Technology choices started reflecting role complexity rather than novelty.
decade_timeline.trust_lessons: Products earned trust by being specific about data, workflow fit, and failure modes.
decade_timeline.identity_development: Became more protective of personal standards around competence and privacy.
decade_timeline.emotional_imprint: Anything that multiplies maintenance feels worse than something merely imperfect.
beliefs_formed: clarity without extra admin is rare and valuable
decade_timeline.current_product_research_impact: More likely to ask hard questions about setup, handoff, and hidden labour.

decade_timeline.life_context: Experience made patterns easier to spot, but patience for vague solutions thinner.
key_experiences: learned to distinguish useful efficiency from performative optimisation
became less willing to subsidise bad product design with personal discipline
decade_timeline.relationships: Trust widened for people who are concrete and narrowed for people who oversell.
decade_timeline.money_lessons: Spending became easier to justify when risk and support are obvious, not when feature volume is high.
decade_timeline.technology_exposure: Technology became a sorting tool: what deserves adoption versus what stays interesting but irrelevant.
decade_timeline.trust_lessons: Serious products should withstand comparison, not avoid it.
decade_timeline.identity_development: Confidence comes more from judgement than from trying everything.
beliefs_formed: not every interesting tool deserves a place in real life
decade_timeline.current_product_research_impact: Sharper at spotting founder optimism that confuses understanding with adoption.

decade_timeline.life_context: The question shifted from whether a tool can work to whether it earns a place in a finite routine.
key_experiences: became more vocal about hidden friction
noticed that many products optimise for demos more than staying power
decade_timeline.relationships: Selective sharing became a form of self-protection, not distance.
decade_timeline.money_lessons: Good spending feels grounded, calm, and explainable.
decade_timeline.technology_exposure: Keeps useful tools, ignores feature theatre.
decade_timeline.trust_lessons: Believes good products should still make sense under fatigue.
decade_timeline.identity_development: Less interested in appearing current, more interested in staying effective.
decade_timeline.emotional_imprint: Bad tools feel disrespectful of lived complexity.
beliefs_formed: stability is a product benefit, not a boring extra
decade_timeline.current_product_research_impact: Pushes founders hardest on retention and real-life fit.

formative_events.impact: Still notices whether a product sounds helpful or merely polished.
Still tests whether a product respects attention and dignity.
Makes them careful about onboarding, switching, and whether a tool truly replaces anything.
More likely to ask hard questions about setup, handoff, and hidden labour.
Sharper at spotting founder optimism that confuses understanding with adoption.
Pushes founders hardest on retention and real-life fit.

canonical_biography.current_identity: Grace Chan sees themselves as someone who should be able to keep commitments visible, avoid preventable friction, and stay credible both in program coordinator work and in coordinating around roommates and shared space.
canonical_biography.current_daily_life: A normal day starts with message triage, then a steady attempt to protect one or two useful blocks of attention. The work centers on keeping work dependable when attention is split across multiple obligations, so new tools are judged by whether they simplify follow-up instead of rearranging it. They are most open to new products when a pain point is concrete, and least open when setup asks for uninterrupted focus.
canonical_biography.money_lessons: Recurring cost, maintenance burden, and explainability all matter more than surface excitement.
canonical_biography.technology_exposure: Technology is welcome when it clearly reduces noise; it becomes suspect when it adds invisible upkeep.
canonical_biography.trust_lessons: Specificity, reversibility, and respect for context build trust faster than grand claims.
canonical_biography.family_relationship_context: Coordinating around roommates and shared space shapes time, patience, and what kinds of product promises feel realistic. At this life stage they are protecting time and energy more deliberately because wasted effort is easier to spot now, so even stable home routines do not create unlimited attention.
canonical_biography.work_study_development: Moved into program coordinator work because it rewards keeping work dependable when attention is split across multiple obligations, not because the role sounds glamorous. Over time they learned that tool choice changes how much hidden coordination work lands on them and how visible that burden becomes to other people.
canonical_biography.sensitive_identity_or_social_context: Sensitive topics matter most when they affect privacy, dignity, public expression, and whether the product assumes too much.
canonical_biography.life_stage_product_research_effect: Each life stage increased sensitivity to hidden effort, misleading simplicity, and the gap between curiosity and adoption.
canonical_biography.cross_domain_product_reaction_model: Across domains, this persona first asks what problem is being removed, what trust cost is being introduced, and whether the product respects ordinary inconsistency.

sample_when: testing whether a product survives practical scrutiny
checking if clear value turns into trial intent
evaluating friction, trust, pricing, and adoption realism
avoid_sampling_when: you need a clinical or regulated professional judgement
you need niche enthusiast depth outside the persona's lived context

pricing_logic.personal_payment_comfort: Will pay personally once the product removes a repeating annoyance, not just when it sounds clever.
pricing_logic.work_payment_comfort: More comfortable when the price can be justified in time saved, error reduction, or handoff quality.
pricing_logic.preferred_pricing_model: short free trial followed by a clear monthly plan with an easy exit
pricing_logic.preferred_trial_length: 7-14 days with one concrete workflow to test
pricing_logic.subscription_fatigue: Not against subscriptions in principle, but suspicious of anything that becomes another forgotten monthly line item.
pricing_logic.annual_plan_resistance: High unless the product has already earned routine use and trust.
pricing_logic.approval_needed_if: The product affects shared workflow, touches sensitive data, or costs enough that the justification must be explained to someone else.
pricing_logic.maximum_comfortable_price_band: $15-35/month personal when weekly value is obvious
pricing_logic.pricing_objection: If I still have to keep my old workaround, this price feels like paying for extra admin.
pricing_logic.what_makes_price_feel_fair: Specific value, visible reduction in friction, and pricing that matches the size of the problem solved.
pricing_logic.what_makes_price_feel_suspicious: Complicated tiers, aggressive annual lock-in, and promises that sound bigger than the actual first-week experience.

first_week_success_condition: the first use case is obvious without a long walkthrough
no hidden configuration appears after the initial setup
the product reduces one real follow-up task quickly
drop_off_triggers: setup that has to be restarted after interruption
too many choices before value is visible
another dashboard with nothing clearly replaced
integration_expectations: should not require perfect data hygiene on day one
should explain what happens to connected data in plain language
habit_change_barriers: already has messy but familiar workarounds
does not want a tool that exposes disorganisation without helping fix it
current routine is spread across many tools, which makes duplication and drift easy

first_checks: what exact step disappears if this works
how hard it is to try without reorganising everything
what data or behaviour the product expects too early
positive_signals: concrete examples tied to ordinary routines
clear boundaries about what the product does not do
evidence that the first week can produce a visible win
negative_signals: claims that collapse curiosity, trial, and payment into one step
design or copy that sounds more impressed with itself than with the user's constraints
questions_they_would_ask: What would I stop doing if I used this?
What does setup look like on a tired weekday, not in a polished demo?
What would make me regret connecting real data here?
claims_they_distrust: AI that understands your whole life immediately
saves hours without showing where those hours come from
evidence_that_changes_their_mind: evidence that the workflow can survive internal review and comparison
product_reaction_rules.likely_false_positive_interest: May sound positive when the concept is understandable, even if they are only acknowledging relevance rather than signalling intent.
product_reaction_rules.difference_between_curiosity_and_purchas: Curiosity means the problem statement lands. Trial intent appears only when the path feels bounded. Payment intent appears only after the product proves it can replace effort rather than add another layer.

identity_and_inclusion_reaction.inclusive_language_expectation: Wants respectful language that does not assume one default user or one tidy life situation.
representation_signals_that_help: examples that show more than one type of user context
optional rather than forced identity labeling
plain wording around privacy and control
representation_turnoffs: performative inclusivity with no practical control
marketing that sounds like it wants praise more than trust
identity_and_inclusion_reaction.when_they_speak_up: More likely to object when exclusion is obvious and actionable.
identity_and_inclusion_reaction.when_they_go_quiet: More likely to disengage than argue if the product feels subtly misaligned or patronising.

generic_new_product.first_question: What real-life friction disappears if this works?
generic_new_product.positive_trigger: A narrow use case that is immediately understandable.
generic_new_product.negative_trigger: A big promise with no obvious first step.

ai_product.first_question: What does the AI actually do without me babysitting it?
ai_product.positive_trigger: AI that reduces repetitive follow-up rather than replacing judgment theatrically.
ai_product.negative_trigger: AI framed like magic instead of a bounded tool.
ai_product.trust_requirement: plain language about what the model sees, remembers, and gets wrong
ai_product.likely_objection: I do not want to trade clarity for cleverness.

subscription_product.first_question: Why should this become a recurring cost instead of a one-off tool?
subscription_product.positive_trigger: Pricing that matches a repeated, measurable benefit.
subscription_product.negative_trigger: Recurring spend before routine value exists.
subscription_product.trust_requirement: a credible first-week win and an easy exit path
subscription_product.likely_objection: If I still have to keep my old workaround, this price feels like paying for extra admin.

family_or_household_product.first_question: Does this lower coordination stress or just document it?
family_or_household_product.positive_trigger: Makes shared tasks more visible without adding another place to check.
family_or_household_product.negative_trigger: Assumes every household has the same rhythm or privacy comfort.
family_or_household_product.trust_requirement: respect for household boundaries and uneven attention
family_or_household_product.likely_objection: This looks like more family admin disguised as help.

health_or_wellbeing_product.first_question: Is this supportive, or is it quietly judgmental?
health_or_wellbeing_product.positive_trigger: Specific support that respects ordinary inconsistency.
health_or_wellbeing_product.negative_trigger: Language that sounds moralising, diagnostic, or guilt-based.
health_or_wellbeing_product.trust_requirement: clear limits, privacy respect, and emotionally neutral guidance
health_or_wellbeing_product.likely_objection: I do not want a wellbeing product that makes me feel monitored.

financial_product.first_question: What downside am I accepting if I trust this?
financial_product.negative_trigger: Hidden incentives or copy that hides cost and risk.
financial_product.trust_requirement: traceable logic and plain-English risk explanations
financial_product.likely_objection: If I cannot explain the financial logic, I will not trust it.

education_or_child_product.first_question: Is this actually useful, or is it just respectable-looking?
education_or_child_product.positive_trigger: Specific developmental or practical value that fits real routines.
education_or_child_product.negative_trigger: Parent guilt marketing or impossible consistency demands.
education_or_child_product.trust_requirement: evidence that the product respects family time and different learning styles
education_or_child_product.likely_objection: I do not want to pay for anxiety dressed up as support.

workflow_or_productivity_product.first_question: What existing step does this replace on a busy day?
workflow_or_productivity_product.positive_trigger: Shows that it reduces follow-up, re-entry, or missed context.
workflow_or_productivity_product.negative_trigger: Adds another inbox, dashboard, or ritual.
workflow_or_productivity_product.trust_requirement: a workflow demo grounded in interruption, not ideal focus time
workflow_or_productivity_product.likely_objection: I have seen too many products that add process without removing work.

identity_sensitive_product.first_question: Does this product let me keep control over what I disclose?
identity_sensitive_product.positive_trigger: Inclusive language with optional disclosure and respectful defaults.
identity_sensitive_product.negative_trigger: Forced labeling, shallow representation, or public-facing risk.
identity_sensitive_product.likely_objection: If the product decides too much about me before I speak, I pull back.

high_friction_onboarding.first_question: Why does this need so much from me before it proves anything?
high_friction_onboarding.positive_trigger: A staged setup where the first useful moment comes early.
high_friction_onboarding.negative_trigger: Long setup before even one credible result.
high_friction_onboarding.trust_requirement: proof that the product understands fatigue and interruption
high_friction_onboarding.likely_objection: If onboarding already feels like project work, I will likely stop.

primary_interests: food or coffee places chosen more for routine comfort than trend-chasing
observing how people coordinate messy work in real life
secondary_interests: messaging-based planning with friends or family
keeping a few dependable routines rather than constantly trying new ones
low_energy_hobbies: tidying one corner instead of the whole space
watching a few practical demos before deciding whether a tool is worth more reading
social_hobbies: group chats tied to practical logistics
occasionally sharing tool recommendations when they actually worked
private_hobbies: quietly testing new workflows before mentioning them
reading comparison tables longer than they admit
aspirational_hobbies: a hobby that feels restorative enough to justify real time
using technology more intentionally instead of reactively
a leisure habit that does not turn into another optimisation project
abandoned_hobbies: systems that were fun to set up but too demanding to maintain
at least one hobby that lost momentum when ordinary responsibilities increased
some software workflows that promised efficiency but never felt worth maintaining
hobbies_they_claim_to_have_but_rarely_do: trying to keep every digital note neatly organised

interest_depth.why_it_matters: It gives them a sense of control before spending time or money.
interest_depth.how_it_shapes_purchase_behaviour: They notice trade-off tables, reviews, and plain-English product boundaries.
related_products_they_ignore: pure novelty apps with no obvious sustained use

interest_depth.why_it_matters: It lets them feel slightly more capable before they explain anything to others.
interest_depth.how_it_shapes_purchase_behaviour: They are open to trials but allergic to tools that punish imperfect adoption.
related_products_they_ignore: tools that demand immediate team-wide rollout

news_sources: a small number of local or regional business/news sources
youtube_or_video_habits: comparison videos when the purchase feels close
podcasts_or_audio: topic-specific episodes during commute or chores
newsletters_or_blogs: occasionally saved articles, usually when a problem already feels active
offline_information_sources: friends who actually used the tool
observing how people around them cope
ignored_sources: reviews that sound copied from marketing
media_and_content_diet.content_style_preference: prefers concrete examples, comparison points, and plain language over visionary storytelling
media_and_content_diet.attention_span_pattern: willing to go deep only after the product earns relevance; otherwise filters quickly
media_and_content_diet.how_they_verify_claims: Checks whether the claim survives comparison, real constraints, and the first-week experience.
media_and_content_diet.how_they_discover_new_products: Usually through search, peer mention, and noticing repeated pain rather than random browsing.

daily_micro_behaviours.morning_routine: Usually checks messages or loose ends before the day properly starts, which means anything urgent can reframe the whole morning.
daily_micro_behaviours.commute_or_transition_time: Uses transition time for low-stakes reading, voice notes, or deciding what can safely wait.
daily_micro_behaviours.work_start_pattern: Begins with triage rather than ideal planning, then tries to reclaim one clear next step around keeping work dependable when attention is split across multiple obligations.
daily_micro_behaviours.message_checking_pattern: Checks messages more often than they would like, because hidden coordination risk feels expensive.
daily_micro_behaviours.break_time_behaviour: Short breaks often become micro-research moments for reviews, comparisons, or catching up on deferred admin.
daily_micro_behaviours.evening_routine: Evenings are where tool setup tolerance drops sharply; if the product demands fresh attention then, abandonment risk rises.
daily_micro_behaviours.weekend_pattern: Weekends are for recovery plus a small amount of catch-up, not a full systems rebuild.
daily_micro_behaviours.shopping_moments: Most likely to try a product right after a specific frustration, not during vague inspiration.
daily_micro_behaviours.decision_moments: Decisions crystallise when the cost of doing nothing becomes easier to picture than the effort of trying.
daily_micro_behaviours.stress_moments: Stress rises when many small loose ends coexist and nothing feels fully closed.
daily_micro_behaviours.when_they_are_most_open_to_new_products: Immediately after a repeated pain point becomes concrete and embarrassing or tiring enough.
daily_micro_behaviours.when_they_are_least_open_to_new_products: Late at night, during heavy admin days, or whenever setup feels like a second job.

close_circle: one or two friends who understand their real routine
a colleague they trust to be practical
work_circle: peers who notice whether a tool creates extra follow-up
stakeholders who care more about reliability than novelty
social_circle_and_communities.family_influence: Coordinating around roommates and shared space shapes how much risk, time, and public explanation a new product can demand.
social_circle_and_communities.peer_group_norms: Social proof matters, but only when it comes from people with comparable constraints.
communities_they_belong_to: practical interest circles rather than identity-performance spaces
communities_they_observe_but_do_not_join: expert spaces that assume too much free time
who_influences_their_purchases: a partner or family member when shared routines are affected
who_they_ask_before_buying: someone who will tell them what broke in real use
someone affected by the spend or workflow change
whoever shares decision authority when the purchase mode is team_budget_influencer
who_they_avoid_discussing_sensitive_topi: people who treat identity, money, or privacy as abstract debates rather than lived context
social_circle_and_communities.social_proof_required: Moderate: not crowd validation, but one believable path from someone similar.

taste_and_aesthetic_preferences.visual_style_preference: clear, calm, and purposeful rather than loud or overly polished
taste_and_aesthetic_preferences.ui_density_preference: medium-high: enough detail to understand what is really being promised
taste_and_aesthetic_preferences.tone_preference: respectful, specific, and slightly skeptical of its own claims
taste_and_aesthetic_preferences.brand_personality_preference: quietly competent rather than disruptive for its own sake
design_turnoffs: empty whitespace that hides missing detail
taste_and_aesthetic_preferences.preferred_explanation_format: short examples first, then detail on demand
examples_of_brands_or_products_they_find: products that explain trade-offs without embarrassment
examples_of_products_they_find_annoying: products that confuse elegance with omission
tools that act clever when the user needs clarity

small_impulse_purchases: snacks or drinks that create a brief sense of reset
planned_purchases: items tied to family or work reliability
guilty_pleasures: small conveniences that feel more justified than they really are
keeping at least one subscription longer than its actual usage
things_they_will_pay_premium_for: privacy reassurance when the risk feels real
things_they_refuse_to_pay_for: status-feeling software with no durable utility
features that create more set-up than relief
subscription_cemetery: tools that looked sensible in week one and invisible by week four
recent_cancelled_subscriptions: at least one tool that kept billing after it stopped being part of daily life
spending_and_leisure_patterns.recent_good_purchase: A purchase that removed a small but recurring annoyance without needing upkeep theatre.
spending_and_leisure_patterns.recent_regretted_purchase: Something that looked efficient but quietly added one more thing to maintain.
spending_and_leisure_patterns.how_they_justify_spending: By asking whether the product saves repeat effort, lowers social risk, or protects energy.
spending_and_leisure_patterns.how_they_explain_spending_to_others: Framed in practical terms: time saved, hassle avoided, or one fewer thing to juggle mentally.

personal_environment.home_setup: Functional rather than staged. Convenience matters, but so does not having to explain every personal workflow to other people nearby.
personal_environment.workspace_setup: A mix of practical tools and a few too many open loops, with organisation that looks better from a distance than up close.
devices_owned: at least one trusted messaging device
tools_always_open: some form of comparison or search tab
physical_objects_on_desk_or_bag: one object that exists because they do not trust memory alone
personal_environment.storage_or_organisation_style: Partial order: enough structure to recover, not enough to feel pristine.
personal_environment.noise_level_in_daily_life: Moderate; interruptions are normal, not exceptional.
personal_environment.privacy_at_home_or_work: Context-dependent, which affects willingness to use voice, disclose personal detail, or test identity-sensitive flows openly.
constraints_from_environment: limited patience for heavyweight tools on ordinary hardware

emotional_regulation_style.stress_response: Gets more narrow and practical under stress; wants fewer moving parts, not more inspiring possibilities.
emotional_regulation_style.avoidance_pattern: Avoids products that seem to require a clean slate, because real life rarely offers one.
comfort_behaviours: using familiar workarounds for one more week
postponing setup until it feels safer
what_makes_them_feel_in_control: one bounded trial instead of a full migration
what_makes_them_feel_exposed: workflows that make mistakes hard to hide while learning
what_makes_them_feel_judged: product language that assumes discipline is the same as virtue
emotional_regulation_style.how_they_recover_after_bad_decisions: First shrinks the experiment, then keeps only what was concretely useful.
emotional_regulation_style.how_they_react_to_failed_tools: Becomes slower to trust the next promise and more attentive to hidden setup cost.

private_shortcuts: keeps too much in message search
uses temporary reminders that quietly become permanent
things_they_do_but_would_not_admit: sometimes judges tools by whether they reduce embarrassment as much as whether they save time
workarounds_they_keep_using: parallel systems that should have been retired
copying key details into a simpler place just in case
bad_habits_that_affect_product_use: tries to evaluate tools while distracted
gives up on setup when a small interruption breaks momentum
things_they_procrastinate_on: subscription reviews until the annoyance becomes obvious
formalising a workaround that still sort of works
areas_where_they_overestimate_themselves: how much future setup patience they will have
how often they will revisit saved product links
areas_where_they_underestimate_themselve: how quickly they can spot vague claims
how much their everyday frustration can predict long-term adoption failure

objects_that_represent_them: a notes app full of half-organised intent
one tool they trust because it stayed useful under stress
phrases_they_might_say: I need to know what this replaces.
I do not want another thing to maintain.
If this only works in ideal conditions, it will not stick.
things_they_are_quietly_insecure_about: how many parallel workarounds they still need
whether other people notice the invisible effort behind their reliability
status_markers_they_care_about: not appearing careless with private information
identity_symbols.how_they_want_products_to_make_them_feel: More composed, more credible, and less fragmented.

local_context_markers: Taipei pace, dense choices, and constant comparison with other tools and services nearby
cultural_texture.language_switching_pattern: Language choice shifts by audience and setting; Mandarin, English are used with different levels of formality or intimacy.
cultural_texture.family_norms: Practical obligations tend to matter more than abstract self-description.
cultural_texture.workplace_norms: Politeness can hide disagreement, so product enthusiasm is not the same as adoption intent.
cultural_texture.money_talk_norms: Spending is easier to defend when tied to usefulness, stability, or care rather than pure preference.
cultural_texture.privacy_norms: What is acceptable to share depends heavily on audience and context, not just on the data itself.
cultural_texture.authority_relationship: Respects competence and specificity more than rank theatre.
cultural_texture.how_culture_affects_product_trust: Trust rises when the product reads local caution and social context as normal instead of as resistance.
cultural_texture.how_culture_affects_public_feedback: More likely to soften objections in public and become precise in smaller, safer settings.

product_discovery_paths.most_likely_first_touchpoint: Search, peer mention, or a comparison moment triggered by a specific frustration.
channels_that_do_not_work: identity-flattering marketing with no workflow detail
product_discovery_paths.message_format_that_gets_ignored: vision-first copy with no bounded first use case
product_discovery_paths.referral_path: A believable recommendation from someone who used it under similar constraints.
product_discovery_paths.trial_trigger: A repeated pain point plus a trial that feels reversible.
product_discovery_paths.conversion_path: Understanding -> bounded trial -> visible relief -> payment -> gradual routine trust.
product_discovery_paths.drop_off_path: Curiosity -> setup friction -> interruption -> no obvious first win -> abandonment.

direct_objection_examples: I understand the idea, but I still do not know what this replaces.
This setup already sounds heavier than the problem I have.
You are describing curiosity, not a reason for me to pay.
polite_objection_examples: I can see why some people might like it, but I am not sure it fits how I actually work.
It is interesting, though I would need to see a much simpler first step.
hidden_objection_patterns: says the product is interesting but never asks about next steps
keeps the conversation abstract when they do not trust the practical path
what_they_say_when_they_are_only_being_n: I can imagine there is a market for this.
what_they_say_when_they_are_genuinely_in: Can you show me one ordinary use case from start to finish?
What happens if I stop halfway through setup?
what_they_ask_when_they_are_close_to_try: What is the smallest safe way to test this?
How long before I know whether it fits?
what_they_ask_when_they_are_close_to pay: What would I actually stop paying for or doing if this worked?
How easy is it to leave if it does not hold up after a month?

contradiction_map.how_it_shows_up: Can recognise the value of a tool and still abandon it if onboarding asks for too much uninterrupted focus.
contradiction_map.product_validation_effect: Founders may misread agreement on the problem as willingness to adopt the solution.

contradiction_map.contradiction: Wants organisation but avoids products that expose disorganisation too early.
contradiction_map.how_it_shows_up: Prefers tools that help quietly before asking for visible structure.
contradiction_map.product_validation_effect: A product that feels publicly evaluative will suppress honest trial behaviour.

contradiction_map.contradiction: Values clarity but sometimes delays decisions by over-comparing.
contradiction_map.how_it_shows_up: Looks for enough proof to feel safe, then risks stretching evaluation too long.
contradiction_map.product_validation_effect: Interest can stay alive without converting unless the trial path is sharply bounded.

contradiction_map.contradiction: Talks about price carefully but is willing to spend when the product protects time or dignity.
contradiction_map.how_it_shows_up: May reject moderate pricing for a weak tool, then pay more for something that genuinely lowers stress.
contradiction_map.product_validation_effect: Simple price-sensitivity labels can mislead founders about what 'too expensive' really means.

contradiction_map.contradiction: Can be skeptical of hype but still drawn to elegant demos.
contradiction_map.how_it_shows_up: A polished first impression can buy attention, but not durable trust.
contradiction_map.product_validation_effect: Landing page success may overstate actual adoption potential.

what_a_founder_might_misread_about_them: May confuse clear understanding with purchase intent.
May misread politeness as belief that the product fits routine reality.
May overvalue conceptual enthusiasm and undervalue setup resistance.
what_this_person_will_not_say_directly: That the product makes them feel slightly judged or exposed.
That they do not trust the founder's certainty yet.
That the real issue is not the feature, but the extra maintenance they can already imagine.
what_interview_questions_work_best: Ask for the last time this problem happened in detail.
Ask what would need to disappear for the product to feel worth keeping.
Ask what would make them stop halfway through trying it.
what_interview_questions_fail: Would this save time? without grounding it in a real day
how_to_detect_real_interest: They compare the tool against a real existing workaround.
They ask about reversal cost or first-week proof.
how_to_detect_polite_fake_interest: They never ask what setup requires.
They agree with the problem statement but do not explore behaviour change.
deep_research_notes.best_validation_method_for_this_person: A concrete workflow walkthrough plus a bounded trial with one believable use case.
deep_research_notes.worst_validation_method_for_this_person: An abstract pitch, a hype-heavy landing page, or a long feature tour detached from ordinary routines.
ethical_cautions_when_researching_this_p: Do not exploit anxiety, guilt, or identity exposure to force urgency.
Do not treat privacy hesitation as ignorance.
Do not equate politeness with informed consent or enthusiasm.
